news_summary: Today's positive news highlights the growing impact of engaging personal storytelling in science communication and the excitement around fresh, accessible popular science books enriching public understanding.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui soulignent l'impact croissant de l'utilisation de récits personnels dans la communication scientifique et l'engouement suscité par les ouvrages de vulgarisation scientifique novateurs et accessibles qui enrichissent la compréhension du grand public.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy ponen de relieve el creciente impacto de la narración personal en la comunicación científica y el entusiasmo que suscitan los libros de divulgación científica novedosos y accesibles que enriquecen la comprensión del público.

articles.title: What a viral TikTok taught me about personal storytelling in science
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-025-04156-4The success of a student’s educational video made me rethink the ways that teaching can create moments of wonder that technology can’t replace.
articles.content:
In October last year, psychology student Jazz Nuku posted a video to the social-media platform TikTok explaining the overlap that she had observed between evolution, physics, psychology, religion and the perception of reality.Credit: Jazz Nuku, Prasert Krainukul/Getty

One evening a few months ago, I opened an e-mail from a former student. She had made a short video about consciousness and perception and posted it on the social-media platform TikTok. It had gone viral, racking up four million views and thousands of comments since its release in October, many of them serious discussions about the ideas she had shared.

I watched it expecting something polished. Instead, I saw hand-drawn pictures: overlapping circles showing how consciousness might sit at the intersection of quantum physics, evolutionary biology, psychology and religion. Her language was unscripted. Her leaps were bold. She was trying to convey something I remembered feeling as a psychology student myself — the moment when the gap between perception and reality truly hits home, and you realize that your own mind is not what you thought it was.

What moved me wasn’t her scientific precision. It was the wonder in her voice. She wasn’t explaining for marks. She was marvelling, out loud, to strangers on the Internet and somehow that marvel had travelled through the screen to millions of people, who stopped scrolling and started thinking.

TikTok was perhaps the last place I expected to find serious discussion about biological psychology and consciousness. But here it was. And watching her, something clicked for me: maybe what we can offer as science educators is not information itself, but the excitement and meaning we derive from the topics we teach. And we can hope that our students will carry that wonder with them into the world.

Teaching through personal stories

Over the past three years, since the public release of OpenAI’s artificial-intelligence chatbot ChatGPT, I have been rethinking the strategies that I use to keep the students in my large undergraduate courses engaged, and prioritizing what they might gain from learning with a human. A staff meeting made me realize that I wasn’t alone in re-evaluating my teaching methods: my colleagues and I gathered to share how our experiences of teaching had changed since the use of generative AI models became widespread.

In the meeting, the mood was heavy. One colleague was visibly upset. A student had told him that they trusted an AI-generated explanation more than his own. ‘I don’t know what to do any more’ was a sentiment that was shared by most of us, including myself. We all felt that students were slipping away. The engagement that I used to take for granted had become harder to find.

I teach courses on biological psychology, which many psychology students approach with dread — it’s heavy on technical terms and mechanisms, and the content can feel distant from their lives. Making this material feel engaging was a challenge long before AI models arrived. But the use of these tools has raised the question ‘what can I offer that a chatbot can’t?’. My answer is twofold: I can provide secure assessment formats such as oral exams, and I can encourage students to relate their coursework to their personal values and experiences.

Creating YouTube and TikTok videos is improving my lab leadership

For their final assignment, I ask my students to choose a topic that they personally connect with. A student struggling with insomnia might explore the relationship between sleep and cognition. A student from a particular cultural background might want to investigate a genetic trait that is common in people who share their heritage. The students develop these ideas throughout the year, getting feedback from their tutors and eventually giving a presentation on their topic and answering questions.

I also try to find my own personal connections to the topics I cover in lectures. When I teach reproductive behaviour, I share how fiercely protective I felt of my child after they were born, and that I experienced flashes of aggression, even towards my husband, in those early days. I tell my students about how understanding oxytocin’s role in maternal behaviour helped me to make sense of what was happening in my own brain. They often laugh at these moments. But I think that students also remember anecdotes such as this one long after they’ve forgotten the definitions in their textbooks.

The moment of wonder
articles.reasoning: The article highlights an inspiring educational approach where personal storytelling and authentic wonder in science teaching engage and inspire a broad audience, including millions on social media. It shows a meaningful positive impact on science education and student engagement, addressing challenges posed by AI tools and fostering deep connection and curiosity in learning.
articles.personality_title: How a viral TikTok video is changing science teaching
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A psychology student named Jazz Nuku made a short video on TikTok about consciousness and how it connects ideas from different sciences. The video became very popular, with millions of views and many people discussing its ideas.

**What happened** – Jazz’s video used simple drawings and honest, personal storytelling instead of polished facts. She shared her wonder about how our minds work, mixing ideas from physics, evolution, psychology, and religion. This caught the attention of her former teacher, who realized that sharing excitement and personal stories can make science more interesting.

**Impact** – This story shows a new way to teach science that goes beyond facts and tests. The teacher noticed that students often find biology and psychology hard and boring, especially with AI tools making information easy to get. But personal stories and real feelings help students connect with the material and remember it better.

**What’s next step** – The teacher now asks students to explore topics that matter to them personally and share their own stories. She also uses videos to share her experiences. This approach helps students stay curious and engaged, even with new technology like AI around.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sharing personal stories and wonder in science teaching helps students connect deeply with learning, even in a world full of easy-to-access AI information.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment une vidéo TikTok virale transforme l'enseignement des sciences
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une étudiante en psychologie, Jazz Nuku, a créé une courte vidéo sur TikTok expliquant la conscience en reliant plusieurs domaines scientifiques. La vidéo est devenue très populaire, avec des millions de vues et beaucoup de discussions.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La vidéo de Jazz utilisait des dessins simples et un récit personnel sincère plutôt que des faits très préparés. Elle a partagé son émerveillement sur le fonctionnement de l'esprit, mélangeant physique, évolution, psychologie et religion. Son ancien professeur a compris que partager son enthousiasme rend la science plus intéressante.

**Impact** – Cette histoire montre une nouvelle façon d’enseigner la science, qui va au-delà des faits et des tests. Le professeur a remarqué que les étudiants trouvent souvent la biologie et la psychologie difficiles et ennuyeuses, surtout avec les outils d’IA qui facilitent l’accès à l’information. Mais les histoires personnelles aident les étudiants à mieux se connecter et retenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Le professeur demande maintenant aux étudiants de choisir des sujets qui les touchent personnellement et de raconter leurs propres histoires. Elle utilise aussi des vidéos pour partager ses expériences. Cette méthode aide les étudiants à rester curieux, même avec la présence de l’IA.

**En une phrase** – Partager des histoires personnelles et de l’émerveillement dans l’enseignement des sciences aide les étudiants à s’impliquer profondément, même dans un monde rempli d’informations faciles grâce à l’IA.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo un video viral de TikTok está cambiando la enseñanza de la ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Una estudiante de psicología llamada Jazz Nuku creó un video corto en TikTok sobre la conciencia y cómo conecta ideas de diferentes ciencias. El video se hizo muy popular, con millones de vistas y muchas personas discutiendo sus ideas.

**Qué pasó** – El video de Jazz usó dibujos simples y una narración personal honesta en lugar de datos pulidos. Compartió su asombro sobre cómo funciona la mente, mezclando física, evolución, psicología y religión. Su antiguo profesor se dio cuenta de que compartir entusiasmo y historias personales puede hacer que la ciencia sea más interesante.

**Impacto** – Esta historia muestra una nueva forma de enseñar ciencia que va más allá de hechos y exámenes. El profesor notó que los estudiantes a menudo encuentran la biología y la psicología difíciles y aburridas, especialmente con herramientas de IA que facilitan obtener información. Pero las historias personales y los sentimientos reales ayudan a los estudiantes a conectarse mejor con el material y recordarlo.

**Próximo paso** – El profesor ahora pide a los estudiantes que exploren temas que les importen personalmente y compartan sus propias historias. También usa videos para compartir sus experiencias. Este enfoque ayuda a los estudiantes a mantenerse curiosos y comprometidos, incluso con la tecnología nueva como la IA.

**Resumen en una frase** – Compartir historias personales y asombro en la enseñanza de la ciencia ayuda a los estudiantes a conectar profundamente con el aprendizaje, incluso en un mundo lleno de información fácil gracias a la IA.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of overlapping translucent circles glowing softly at their intersections, each circle symbolizing evolution, physics, psychology, and religion, with a delicate, hand-drawn style; at the center, a radiant abstract shape representing consciousness and wonder, surrounded by gentle, flowing lines that evoke connection and personal storytelling, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: The best new popular science books of March 2026
articles.summary: A new book from Rebecca Solnit, promising to bring us hope in these “difficult times”, is among our pick of popular science titles out this month – along with a guide on how to talk to AI, and a look at modern warfare
articles.content:
A new book from Rebecca Solnit, promising to bring us hope in these “difficult times”, is among our pick of popular science titles out this month – along with a guide on how to talk to AI, and a look at modern warfare

Rebecca Solnit has a new book out this month Trent Davis Bailey

March, in the northern hemisphere anyway, is about venturing out for some much-needed vitamin D and dodging showers. Forget that – just head for a decent café where you can delve into the marvellous science books we’ve got waiting for you. This month you can explore how animals shaped our world, how to spot liars from their language, what forest trees can tell us – and flowers as revolutionaries. There is some stronger stuff too, if you are in the mood: try AI in the hands of the US military, or a deep cultural look at how our world has changed beyond recognition. Whatever your choice, it’s all guaranteed to enrich the inner you.

Herlands by Megha Mohan

What would a world look like if women made the rules? In one still run largely by men, it’s an interesting question. According to her publishers, author Megha Mohan was inspired by her great-grandmother’s matrilineal community in South India to scour the world in search of “lessons from societies where women make the rules”. Such societies have always existed, with modern micro-examples including South Korea’s unique online feminist trolls, co-housing experiments in Paris and North London and the Rain Queens of South Africa. And what might different ways of collaborating, working, child rearing – above all, power and identity structures – look like in such a world? Mohan– the BBC’s first global gender and identity correspondent in 2018 – explores.

How to Talk to AI (And How Not To) by Jamie Bartlett

Are you getting the best out of AI? Assuming you have increasingly little choice in the matter, it’s probably a plan to buckle down and read up. To judge by Jamie Bartlett’s earlier work, especially The Dark Net, How to Talk to AI promises to deliver on the nitty-gritty of how AI thinks and reasons and the best ways to exploit its (sorry) super-human abilities. Expect to learn how some folks are turbo-charging work and everyday life with AI, while others are falling down conspiracy rabbit holes and/or experiencing psychosis.

Advertisement

When the Forest Breathes by Suzanne Simard

It’s a fair claim to say (as her publisher does) that Suzanne Simard has helped transform our understanding of the profound intelligence and interconnectedness of trees. The bestselling author of Finding the Mother Tree, she is professor of forest ecology at the University of British Columbia, Canada, where she leads The Mother Tree Project – and has a global reputation for research on tree connectivity and communication and its impact on the health and diversity of forests. Her new book, When the Forest Breathes, taps into the deep-rooted cycles of renewal that sustain the forest and how they can also help us to protect the world’s ecosystem. Simard grew up in British Columbia, in a family of loggers committed to sustainable stewardship, so her life has been a very singularly committed one – which often makes for a great book. Here’s hoping.

Animate by Michael Bond

Michael Bond is a former New Scientist staffer and author of a growing pile of books exploring the inner world of how we shape each other (peer pressure, fans, belonging) and the outer world (wayfaring and his own family’s part in settling the Canadian prairies). This time he sets off on a connected but different track, exploring how animals shaped our minds and cultures, “from our hunter-gatherer ancestors whose brains were rewired by the prey they hunted and the predators they feared, to the medieval and Enlightenment thinkers who used animals to promote notions of human supremacy”. If everything that was thought to make us human is shared with other creatures, who are we and what is our place in the world? What is the new order? Looking forward to this one.

The Language of Lies by Kirsty King

Can you spot a liar, or separate truth from fiction? Who do you trust in these mendacious, deepfake days? Forensic psychologist Kirsty King may have a new way to help us weave our way through the lies we all tell to keep our lives going, and the bigger ones that are extremely damaging. We need all the help we can get here, given the failure of other approaches such as physiology (think micro-expressions and the like). So, can lies be exposed by paying close attention to the language liars use? Drawing on research from forensic linguistics and psychology, King shares real-life case studies and stories to explore the “tells”. Should be a fascinating read.

A tea plant – as featured in David George Haskell’s new book Blickwinkel / Alamy

How Flowers Made Our World by David George Haskell

It’s a big claim: without flowers, human beings would not exist. But sounds like environmental scientist David George Haskell can back up the publishing hype in How Flowers Made Our World – subtitled “The story of nature’s revolutionaries”. He delves into everything from the “fascinating but less celebrated flowers such as seagrasses and tea to show us what we’ve been missing”, to the power of plants as inventive agents, able to “build and sustain rainforests, savannahs, prairies; and even ocean shores”. Looking to the future, he says that flowers “offer us lessons on resilience and creativity in the face of rapid environmental change”. Lots to celebrate there then.

The Beginning Comes After the End by Rebecca Solnit

We may not have the world promised by Star Trek and the like, but anyone living in a sealed off bunker for the past fifty or sixty years would still emerge into the sunlight blinking at the political landscape of the 21st century. Rebecca Solnit has been at the forefront of thinking about this for quite a while, winning plaudits and nominations for book awards as she goes. Her latest, The Beginning Comes After the End, her publisher says, “is a culmination of years of activism and offers a unique perspective on our politics and our humanity, to give hope in difficult times and to urgently remind us that the power to change the world is within our reach”. Let’s hope so.

On the Origin of Sex by Lixing Sun

What’s not to like in a book about sex? Even better, a book about sex in animals– which promises to tell “the weird and wonderful science of how our planet is populated”. This is one of New Scientist’s 2026 books to watch out for, and its author, Lixing Sun, is a professor of biology at Central Washington University. A sneak peek reveals, among much else, that the female mole is a “true rebel of the animal kingdom” with both ovaries and testes – and that California condors are capable of immaculate conception.

Project Maven by Katrina Manson

Could this book be any more timely? Project Maven by Katrina Manson is a kind-of briefing for the hell we see on our screens every night as Operation Epic Fury unfolds in the Middle East. Manson tells the chilling story of how the US Department of Defense launched Project Maven in 2017– an initiative designed to harness artificial intelligence for military targeting. She is a Bloomberg reporter who covers national security and cutting-edge tech, so you can be pretty sure she will know what she’s writing about. This looks to be fascinating and compelling stuff– but you may need a strong stomach.

Inescapable by F. Marina Schauffler

We’re fast getting used to the acronym PFAS to describe per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, and the horrifying global environmental legacy trailing in the wake of what are labelled “forever chemicals”. These invisible, hard-to-remove chemicals are in the blood of most people on Earth, as they permeate everyday life and the natural world. Journalist Marina Schauffler zeros in on Maine, the US’s most north-easterly state. She tells the stories of farmers, firefighters, tribal members, researchers, everyday homeowners and officials as they suffer from, or fight back against, PFAS contamination in a place known for its rich farms, woods and waters – and, apparently, at the forefront of PFAS testing and regulation. The poignant accounts here may be from the US, but it could equally well be somewhere near you.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a curated selection of new popular science books that collectively inspire hope, deepen understanding of nature, AI, gender roles, and environmental challenges, and promote knowledge that can empower societal and personal growth. The books cover meaningful topics with broad relevance, such as environmental stewardship, AI literacy, gender equality, and resilience, providing substantial context and potential positive impact on readers and society.
articles.personality_title: March 2026’s new science books offer fresh ideas on nature, AI, and society
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every March, new books arrive that explore science and society in ways that help us understand the world better. This year’s selection includes topics from how forests communicate, to AI’s role in daily life, and how women’s leadership might shape communities.

**What happened** – In March 2026, several popular science books were published. Rebecca Solnit’s new book offers hope and insights about politics and humanity. Megha Mohan explores societies where women hold power. Jamie Bartlett explains how to interact with AI effectively. Suzanne Simard shares discoveries about how trees connect in forests. Other books cover animal influence on humans, spotting lies, the power of flowers, animal sex, military use of AI, and environmental dangers from “forever chemicals.”

**Impact** – These books give readers clear, interesting ways to learn about important topics. For example, Suzanne Simard’s work helps us see forests as living communities, not just groups of trees. Jamie Bartlett’s guide can help people use AI better and avoid problems. Megha Mohan’s research shows us different ways societies can work when women lead. Together, these books encourage understanding of the natural world, technology, and how people live, helping readers think about change and care for the planet and each other.

**What's next step** – Readers can choose from these books to learn about new ideas and challenges. The authors’ research and stories may inspire more studies, discussions, and actions on subjects like environmental protection, gender equality, and responsible AI use. Some books, like the one on military AI, may raise questions that lead to careful debates about technology and ethics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – March’s popular science books invite readers to explore nature, technology, and society in ways that deepen understanding and encourage thoughtful change.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les nouveaux livres de science populaire de mars 2026 explorent nature, IA et société
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque mois de mars, de nouveaux livres arrivent pour nous aider à mieux comprendre la science et la société. Cette année, les sujets vont de la communication dans les forêts, au rôle de l’IA dans la vie quotidienne, jusqu’à la place des femmes dans le pouvoir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, plusieurs livres de science populaire ont été publiés. Le nouveau livre de Rebecca Solnit offre espoir et réflexions sur la politique et l’humanité. Megha Mohan étudie les sociétés où les femmes dirigent. Jamie Bartlett explique comment bien utiliser l’IA. Suzanne Simard révèle comment les arbres communiquent dans les forêts. D’autres livres parlent de l’influence des animaux, de la détection des mensonges, du pouvoir des fleurs, du sexe animal, de l’IA militaire et des dangers des « produits chimiques éternels ».

**Impact** – Ces livres permettent aux lecteurs d’apprendre clairement et de façon intéressante sur des sujets importants. Par exemple, le travail de Suzanne Simard nous montre que les forêts sont des communautés vivantes, pas seulement des arbres. Le guide de Jamie Bartlett aide à mieux utiliser l’IA et à éviter des problèmes. Les recherches de Megha Mohan montrent comment les sociétés changent quand les femmes dirigent. Ensemble, ces livres encouragent à mieux comprendre la nature, la technologie et la vie en société, et à réfléchir au changement et à la protection de la planète et des humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les lecteurs peuvent choisir ces livres pour découvrir de nouvelles idées et défis. Les recherches et histoires des auteurs pourraient inspirer plus d’études, de discussions et d’actions sur la protection de l’environnement, l’égalité des genres et l’utilisation responsable de l’IA. Certains livres, comme celui sur l’IA militaire, pourraient provoquer des débats sur la technologie et l’éthique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les livres de science populaire de mars invitent à explorer la nature, la technologie et la société pour mieux comprendre et encourager des changements réfléchis.
articles.personality_title_es: Los nuevos libros de ciencia popular de marzo 2026 exploran naturaleza, IA y sociedad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada marzo llegan nuevos libros que nos ayudan a entender mejor la ciencia y la sociedad. Este año, los temas van desde cómo se comunican los bosques, el papel de la IA en la vida diaria, hasta cómo sería una sociedad gobernada por mujeres.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026 se publicaron varios libros de ciencia popular. El nuevo libro de Rebecca Solnit ofrece esperanza y reflexiones sobre la política y la humanidad. Megha Mohan investiga sociedades donde las mujeres tienen el poder. Jamie Bartlett explica cómo usar la IA de forma efectiva. Suzanne Simard comparte descubrimientos sobre la conexión entre árboles en los bosques. Otros libros hablan sobre la influencia de los animales, cómo detectar mentiras, el poder de las flores, el sexo en animales, el uso militar de la IA y los peligros de los “químicos eternos.”

**Impacto** – Estos libros dan a los lectores formas claras e interesantes de aprender sobre temas importantes. Por ejemplo, el trabajo de Suzanne Simard nos muestra que los bosques son comunidades vivas, no solo árboles. La guía de Jamie Bartlett ayuda a usar mejor la IA y evitar problemas. La investigación de Megha Mohan muestra cómo cambian las sociedades cuando las mujeres lideran. En conjunto, estos libros fomentan la comprensión de la naturaleza, la tecnología y la vida social, ayudando a pensar en cambios y en cuidar el planeta y a las personas.

**Próximo paso** – Los lectores pueden elegir estos libros para aprender nuevas ideas y desafíos. Las investigaciones y relatos de los autores podrían inspirar más estudios, debates y acciones sobre la protección ambiental, la igualdad de género y el uso responsable de la IA. Algunos libros, como el de IA militar, pueden generar debates sobre tecnología y ética.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los libros de ciencia popular de marzo invitan a explorar la naturaleza, la tecnología y la sociedad para entender mejor y fomentar cambios pensados.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy café scene with an open book glowing softly on a wooden table, surrounded by symbolic elements from the featured books: a network of intertwined roots and branches representing forest communication, a delicate tea plant with blossoms symbolizing flowers’ influence, and a subtle digital circuit pattern blending into the background to evoke AI, all rendered in warm, natural tones with detailed brushstrokes.
